captions.text: Welcome to Agent Memory, building Memory-Aware Agents,
built in partnership with Oracle.
Many developers building with LLMs have run into the scenario
of an agent that works in a single session,
but loses or forgets everything it learned when the session ends.
Memory helps turn a stateless agent into one
that can overcome this challenge.
In this course, you'll learn how to engineer memory systems
that give agents persistence, continuity,
and the ability to learn over time
using Oracle AI database as the infrastructure layer
for storing and retrieving agent memory.
I'm delighted to introduce our instructors for this course,
Richmond Alake and Nacho Martínez.
Thanks, Andrew. I'm excited to work with you
and your team on this. Thanks, Andrew.
Richmond and Nacho will walk you through using a database,
LangChain, and an LLM-powered pipeline
for memory augmentation, extraction, and tool retrieval.
With these, you'll learn how memory-first architectures
address the problem of agents failing at long-horizon tasks.
You build a memory manager
that stores, retrieves, and operates on different memory types.
What excites me most about this course is the shift it represents.
We've spent the last few years
focused on prompt engineering and context engineering,
figuring out how to get the most of a single LLM call.
But for agents that need to operate
over days or weeks, that's not enough.
You need memory engineering, treating long-term memory as first-class infrastructure,
external to the model, persistent and structured.
That's what this course is all about. And by the end,
you will have the tools to build agents that don't just respond,
they remember, improve,
and are aware of their memory capabilities.
And the patterns we teach here aren't theoretical.
We'll walk through real implementations,
building memory stores, wiring up extraction pipelines,
and handling contradictions in memory.
You'll leave with working code that you
can adapt for your own production agents.
Many people have worked to create this course.
I'd like to thank from Oracle, Elif Sema Balcıoğlu,
and from DeepLearning.AI, Brendan Brown.
The first lesson will be an overview of AI agents
and why they need memory.
We'll break down the failure modes of stateless agents
and introduce the memory-first architecture
that the rest of the course builds on.
Oh wait, I forgot. What are we doing again?
All right, memory.
I hope you enjoy the course.
